narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put significant effort into your application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 32, and school deciles of 3rd in both Year 1 and Year 3 grades. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 48.2% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass on their first try. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your academic record that stood out compared to typical applicants. Your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pulling the prediction toward expecting bar passage success, which is a bright spot in your profile since it's below the average of around 3.21. However, your Year 3 school decile rank was the top factor pushing toward a failure prediction—it's in the lower third of your peers, well below the typical 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model a potential struggle in sustaining strong performance. Your LSAT score came next as a notable drag, ranking below the average of 36.7 and contributing meaningfully toward the failure outlook, unlike higher scorers who often tip the scales favorably. Meanwhile, your upper-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans offered some helpful counterbalance, nudging things slightly toward success like they do for many similar students. Overall, while your GPA showed promise, the combination of your lower decile ranks and LSAT pulled harder against you than for the average applicant, who tends to have stronger showings in those areas. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a solid background—many in similar situations improve with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through additional coursework or tutoring, and perhaps gathering letters highlighting your resilience and full-time commitment. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the model's view significantly, opening doors at this or other schools. We're rooting for your success—keep pushing.
